Cuando te preguntan cómo leer una frase con comillas dentro de otras comillas, es como descifrar un acertijo. Primero, ten en cuenta las reglas del juego. Las comillas exteriores son la casa principal y las internas son los invitados. Dependiendo del idioma o la estilo, podemos usar comillas simples ('Hey') o dobles ("¡Hola!"). Entonces, si tienes algo como "Sé que ella dijo 'Voy a la fiesta'.", la primera pareja de comillas dobles son la casa y las simples dentro son los invitados.

¿Por qué hacemos esto? Bueno, es como ponerle una etiqueta de "déjame entrar" a la voz de alguien diferente dentro de una conversación. Sirve para evitar confusiones y para que todo quede claro como el agua.

Pero espera, hay más. Las comillas son como ese gafete que ponemos alrededor de palabras que queremos destacar o que no son de nuestro día a día. Cuando leemos algo como: "El famoso escritor dijo: 'La lluvia en Sevilla...'", es como decir "Oigan, esto es lo que él dijo".

In his book entitled _The Wounded Healer_, Henri Nouwen (1985) talks about the importance of finding our own true worth by acknowledging and healing the wounds we carry around with us. Most of us, he says, are carrying around the belief that we are not enough, we are not good enough, we are not worthy enough. We are thus weighed down by the very human need to prove ourselves to the world and to God. And yet the only way we can find our true worth is by acknowledging and embracing our brokenness ourselves in all of our uncool humanity.

I experienced this broken place of myself over these years as a mother. It is what unlocked my ability to be present with my children. Louisa was a huge part of the reason I did the work I did to find this place of myself. I loved her so much I didn't want to lose her. I didn't want to get stuck without her. It seems terribly simplistic to think that I found my worth by finding my daughter's worth but that is exactly what happened. In honouring her broken heart I found my own worth. In walking with her through her grief I found worth of my own. It is worth noting that I wrote very little of those years. From the first pages about preparation for Louisa's birth all the way through to the first anniversary of her death I was writing constantly. I wrote more about this time than I had at any other period in my life but those years around her death produced few words. Perhaps I was too busy trying to find my way 'back' to my own worth to find my way to the page.

A new study by researchers at the University of Otago is providing fresh insights into how children are able to reason "intuitively," or quickly and with seemingly little mental effort.

The psychology researchers measured children's eye movements using a variety of audio-visual tasks as they learned new concepts.

Research in the Department of Psychology, supervised by Professor Harlene Hayne and led by PhD candidate Hannah Kirk, is the first to test the development of ‘intuitive’ or implicit reasoning ability in children.

It harnesses eye-tracking technology to provide unprecedented insights into how the human mind works – even before children are able to express these ideas verbally.

"Intuitive or implicit reasoning gives humans the ability to reason quickly and with minimal cognitive effort. A classic example is our ability to generate expectations about upcoming events based on minimal information – for instance, we immediately know the sound of a phone ringing is more likely to be followed by a human voice than a dog's bark," Ms Kirk says.

"Intuitive reasoning is present from birth but until now we had little idea of exactly how it develops across childhood."

The researchers measured children's eye movements during four different learning tasks and compared the results to those from a group of adults.

The first two studies investigated whether children learned implicit rules about the relationship between two sounds, such as a dog's bark and a human voice, following a series of pairings.

"We found young children intuitively inferred these rules just as well as adults did but an interesting twist was that children learned the rules more quickly than adults did," Ms Kirk says.

A third study investigated implicit rule learning for shapes on a computer screen.

"Like the earlier studies, we found strong evidence for implicit rule learning in children, indicating they can discover and use abstract rules to guide their expectations about upcoming events but the rate of learning is much faster than in adults," she says.

The fourth study examined implicit memory for sequences of sounds.

"Sound sequences are particularly interesting because people are often unaware of the rules that govern the sequences but they are able to use these rules to infer upcoming sounds," Ms Kirk explains.

"Again we found evidence for implicit learning across all age groups but the children learned much faster than adults over the first five presentations of each sequence – indicating younger children are more efficient implicit learners."

The studies all reveal that what appears to be more effortful for adults is easier for children. Ms Kirk believes the findings provide an important step towards understanding human implicit reasoning and could have significant real-world applications.

"For example, implicit reasoning is the critical first step in scientific discovery but we need to understand how it develops in children before we can design learning environments tailored to children's unique knowledge states and abilities," she says.

Children are naturally curious and have a knack for simplifying things that adults have complicated. They possess a fresh perspective that allows them to approach problems with creativity and simplicity. This is why children are often capable of finding solutions that adults overlook.

One reason children can simplify things is because they are not burdened by the constraints and assumptions that adults carry. They are not influenced by preconceived notions or rigid frameworks. They are open to new ideas and willing to explore unconventional paths.

Children also have a unique ability to ask questions. They are not afraid to seek answers and are constantly inquisitive about the world around them. By asking questions, they challenge the status quo and encourage adults to think differently.

Moreover, children have an innate sense of innocence and purity. They are not jaded by the complexities of the world and can see things in a more straightforward and genuine way. This innocence allows them to find simple solutions to problems that adults may overcomplicate.

It is important for adults to recognize the value of children's perspectives and to learn from them. By embracing simplicity and adopting a childlike curiosity, adults can often find innovative solutions to complex problems.

So, the next time you find yourself struggling with a complicated issue, take a moment to think like a child. Approach the problem with a fresh perspective, ask questions, and seek simplicity. You may be surprised at the insights and solutions that arise.

In conclusion, children are capable of simplifying things that adults have complicated. Their curiosity, innocence, and open-mindedness allow them to find creative and straightforward solutions. Adults can learn a great deal from children by adopting a childlike approach to problem-solving.

Starting with the basics, a kitchen table runner is a long, narrow piece of fabric that is placed down the center of a table. On the other hand, boho table runners are known for their vibrant colors, bold patterns, and eclectic designs. They are ideal for adding a touch of whimsy and creativity to your table.
If you are looking to purchase a kitchen table runner, there are several places where you can find them.
